speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six of the interview with Tola Hauptman. Tola, how did you become reunited with your father? Yes. So after that news, that my father is alive, which we didn't believe, my, my mother went for a long journey, because as you know, it wasn't any communication. Might be occasionally a, you know, a truck or military truck. So she went to Prushkuv and she found out that he's in a, a, a Polish camp. He was in a Polish camp. He was there, brought from the bunker. But over there, they were-- there were sort of type of Polish people, and it was one taxi man, or taxi driver. And at Christmas time, he recognized that my father doesn't sing the carols. And he put a placard on his back, Jude. You know? So everyone could see. And someone came and said, you know what he put it on? Jude. And they tore it off. And then my father noticed a man who was a barber in, in Wawer. And he was-- you know, my father was bald. He was cutting his hair, and my father was regularly going to him. And he didn't have an option. He said to him, look, [? Vladeg, ?] do you recognize me? And he said, no. Look at my head. Do you recognize that head? No. I am Izrael Moszek Strosberg. And he said, really? Oh. And he said, you have to help me, because people are on my back that I am Jewish. And that was some-- you know, another [? avventura. ?] And that man, that Polish man said, I know him. He was my [POLISH]. My [POLISH] is nearly like my family, you know? My in-law, or something like that. And they let him be, you know? After that, because he was treif. He, he, he was, I mean, kosher. But my father was treif, you know? And he was an alibi that my father is Polish. So when your mother went to there, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother went to there, my father, already he, he was installed over there, cooking for the whole group of people who were homeless. And he was quite cheerful, and he didn't realize that his family is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, Antek. Antek was a typical Polish name. I'm looking for Antek. Which one? Oh, he hasn't got an eye. Oh, the blind Antek. So everyone knew who the blind Antek is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they call him, and he said, gee, I'm, I'm here, already, you know-- I'm in good shape. We have plenty to eat. And my mother said, you know, on purpose, [NON-ENGLISH]. So you stay here and your poor children are starving, and so on, so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went with her. And that was how we met again, you know? And we went to our old quarters in Grahovska Street. And my father started to do something about the business, which from the start. But it was sort of a not one man business, but a cooperative.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived modestly. I went to school. [NON-ENGLISH] I took classes in one year, and then I went to the university to study English, which the lectures were in Polish. We used to read literature and so on. That was until I said, we have to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the anti-Semitism, because I saw that, that Polish people become more anti-Semitic, even after the war, because they believe that every Jew has got treasure of some sort, you know? And even the one who helped my father a bit, you know, his son was-- or his daughter was getting married. He said, he, he had a trick to come to my father-- we didn't have anything-- to ask for a dowry towards the daughter. He was-- she was marrying a, a famous writer, you know, son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, you want from me a dowry? How can I give you? I just came from the camp. And he said, you've got money. So he said, you know, I've got the block of land. Bite it. I can't give it to you. And on the other hand, he said, I tell you something. And if I give you some money, some sum, will you invite me to the wedding? And he said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was very sensitive. And after that, I said, we're leaving. And I went with my brother to the military quarters to get a-- you know, from the military service, a, a pass that he's free, because we go in offices.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the officer who was interviewing us, he said, two young people like you not looking Jewish? What for you leaving Poland? You have nothing to worry about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was another stung for me to go, to leave, as soon as possible. I couldn't stand it, to, to be humiliated because I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was pressing very hard on my parents. And they said, yes, you are right. We have to leave. And my mother went to Paris first, because she had a uncle who was single.

speaker: interviewee
text: She inherited something, but it was a long story, because she had cousins in Israel from before the war, and they all wanted a part of it, but no one wanted to put money into getting it, you know, through the courts. So my mother-- but she obtain a permit. And she, as a, as a result, she, she send us permits to go to France. And that's how we came to France. And I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole family? Your father, mother, sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother was already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your father, your brother, and yourself went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And we were reunited with my mother. And my mother send me to a, a Jewish school to learn how to sew, you know? And we were paid from an American joint, or some other organizations. And I finish. Then I went to [? ORT. ?] In the meantime, I met my husband in Alliance Francaise. And we got very soon married. And he applied for the permit and visa, but I got it first. And my parents wanted me to, to send for them, because my father was too old to get a permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to come first.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to-- and I was already advanced pregnant, you see? So I had to go as a single girl, because we were married, but on my permit we didn't want to change anything, because we didn't want to make it more difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was so stupid that I went pregnant. It was, I suppose, nearly seven months pregnant. And I went by myself. Maybe end of six months, something like that. And I went by myself. It was a terrible experience. Coming to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '51. And it was a very hard time, because we had only five pounds. When my husband came, when my son was born, already one week old, and we celebrated on the ship. But I had it really hard, because the birth took place, it was three days and three nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- he was born in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. In a women's-- What was it? Women's Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Royal Women's Hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Royal Women's Hospital. He was born. And-- so I had to go to the relief. It was quite hard for me to beg for a--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the Jewish Relief Organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not for money, but I wanted to have a machine that I could sew. Finally, I got along, and I was working a bit. I was very weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before your husband came over? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My husband was here already, and he was sick, too, because he got pneumonia before he came. And he said that it was a terrible mistake that I went without him. Nobody-- sort of-- he was by himself, and he was neglected. He didn't have-- he couldn't cook or anything. And he came here quite sick, and it took us quite a few years to stand on our feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: My husband, because he finished the same school-- actually, he was studying law in Warsaw before he left. But in France, that wasn't enough. It was nothing, you know? So he had to learn a trade, so he learned the sign. And he went-- we went-- we both were working as machinists, but we couldn't [? handle ?] [? it ?] because we were too slow, you know? But somehow, slowly, slowly. We saved some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with a-- you know, with some loans from people, we got a [? meal ?] [? part ?] for one year. And then we went back to work. And then my husband wanted to sell the house. I said, no. You're not selling the house, because for all the money what we had, I wanted to have a house so I will feel, for once, secure. And-- so he was desperate, and he opened his own business, not knowing anything about it. But first of all, he was a representative of some firm. But he learned, and he opened the business. And he was quite successful, but he didn't overdo it. We were both not greedy. We wanted to have enough to live. And when he was 50, all right, he said, that's enough. Of course, he didn't-- he didn't count that there will be good times and bad times. And-- but we don't complain. We are quite happy with what we got. And that's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And you've got--

speaker: interviewee
text: A daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: A daughter. When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: '57. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your parents? When did they come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came when my son was two years old, or one and a half, something like that. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tola, with all the experiences you've had, especially your experiences during the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it make you feel about people? How do you think it affected how you felt about people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About people in general?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I've got my father's nature, in that respect. I take people, their own, you know, characteristics. And I never judge people as a group. I, I judge them as individuals. There are good people and bad people. Of course, we wouldn't be alive if some people wouldn't help us. But I reckon mostly the anti-Semitism is caused by their religion and very much by not knowing. And it's-- the biggest phenomenon is that there are no Jews in Poland, and there is bigger anti-Semitism that it ever was that I can't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, I must tell you that once I got a-- I get a phone call. Who is it? Krystyna I was-- all the time, I was corresponding to her and helping as much as I could. And she said-- I said, where are you? I said, I'm in Melbourne. And I said, um, it's unreal. I can't believe it. And she said, yes. My son is here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, that was another, you know, another sort of bad experience, that the son was for ten years, and she didn't let me know. [SIGHS] That says for, for itself. Also, it was a nice family. It was a very good family. I was-- at the end, I was quite happy to be there. But it really hurt me that she didn't. And when she left, I help her as much as I could. I gave her money and things. And I said, no to-- we were invited to the son. And I said, now I will replace your mother, and he was smiling. He never rang me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she says, I'm too lazy to write letters. Of course, the parents, who were mainly-- they helped me to survive. But I, I really loved her as a friend. And when she was leaving, she said, ring me. I will ring her in Poland, you know? I said, why? We can correspond. I write to you, you write back. She, she never wrote me. So I sent her-- every Christmas, I sent her something, some money. And I said, you-- reply if you got the money. And she didn't reply. That was last year. And I decided I'm not writing anymore. Finish, you know? I've got my pride.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she doesn't need my money-- that's another thing-- because she's got a flat. She inherited another flat from her parents. She's got a--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Just don't play with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is it? She's got a house in [INAUDIBLE], a holiday place. She invited me. She wanted me to come, but she never sort of was close to me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to her-- she said, why don't you come to Poland? I said, because of some people, even like you, you know? You are a friend to a individual, but you hate my, my race, I said. And that's why I don't go to Poland. How do you feel about religion now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe in any religion. I think it's very bad for humanity. It proves itself, that it's no good for people. Any religion, if it comes to extremes, which it comes, You know? In-- because of religion, many millions of people were killed. From other reasons, too, but mostly religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I believe in something, in some providence, I believe. I, I don't know what it is. I do believe, but I, I believe less than I used to, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to believe, as-- just by myself-- in reincarnation, you know? But yes, I believe in something. There is something. I believe in nature, for instance. I'm against the, the big explosion, you know, all sorts of that stuff. I'm against it. It didn't-- won't do any good for humanity in the long run. Is there anything else you'd like to say for the future? Are there any other final words that you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? That I love people, I still believe in people. And we should do everything to make people understand each other. And we, as Jews, are doing a good job because nobody knew what we are like, and we're letting them know. Because during the stay, my stay at Slawinski's, they were so surprised that I am washing myself every day in cold water, cold, freeing water. Because my father told me, you won't be going, how do you have to wash yourself to, to get, you know, stronger. And they were washing themself only when they were going to the church, which means once a week. So, you know, it's incredible what religion can do, and ignorance. Ignorance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: For being interviewed today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It will--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandfather on my mother's side. A very religious man, and well known in Prague, Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's his wife, my grandmother on my mother side. She was a very clever woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my grandfather on my father's side, who died in a Polish uprising. They were three generations, him, his son, and his grandson, who were called by the Germans and killed in Saxon Garden during the Polish uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wad '43, 1943. That was the-- that was the uprising, Polish uprising. This is my aunt, which I loved. One of the five sisters of my mother, the only one who survived. And this is her second husband, who was her brother-in-law. And it's his son, who was found in the country after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some time after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Early after. This is a photo of me and my husband at the wedding in '51 in Paris, where all my clothes were borrowed, borrowed. Yes. Why it was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, who are the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my parents and my brother after the war. The photo is made in Paris. after I left for Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year do you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to tell you that I made a mistake. My marriage was in 1950, not in '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo is my husband, me, and my little son, Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And around when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old--

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he was born in May. He could have had-- I-- six months, or something. One.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us who the people are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother's wedding. He met his wife here, and his--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right hand, start with the right hand side.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right hand, the lower part is her mother. And that's my daughter, Evelyn. And that's my mother. Then it's Leon, my husband, and myself, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the background now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The background. The couple, the young couple, and they are cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. And Mrs. Pell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '53 or '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Leon's bar mitzvah. We are dressed up and ready to go to the shul. And my family is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '51, So it would be '69, '60-- I'm not good with the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it-- where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In temple bat in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the, in the liberal synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberal.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the right-hand side, can you tell us who are the people here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my brother, much, much bigger now. His wife, his son, Bernie. My cousins from Israel, wife, and Judy Strosberg, who is my niece, married to the Israeli next to her. And my cousin, my first cousin, the lovely [? Arnold. ?] The only child. And their children, three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about three years ago. So what? In '96?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Evelyn, at the graduation. She finish RMIT, and she's an accountant. She hates every minute of it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a question. Can't think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at RMIT.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: That document is a certificate which was very important for-- as a witness to the Germans, because it was about the [? Otto ?] [? Brand, ?] who was the main [INAUDIBLE] in deportation of Jews. He was present when they caught my father hiding people-- mothers with children. And this certificate is in Yad Vashem. And he-- at the same time, he lost his sight because he was beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And sent to the Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get this certificate? Quite recently, because my, my brother and my sister-in-law went to Israel and, and-- to visit their daughter. And they were in Yad Vashem, and they found this certificate. They didn't even know about it. And it was just a year ago, two years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a few months ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
